note: Curated list of AI-related crawlers and control tokens. 'purpose' is one of: training (collects data for model training), search (builds an AI search index), user (fetches pages on behalf of a user's live request), control (a robots.txt token that controls AI use without being a crawler itself). Corrections welcome via pull request; please link vendor documentation.

crawlers.notes: Collects public web data that may be used to train OpenAI models.
Builds the index behind ChatGPT search features. Blocking it removes you from those results without affecting training.
Fetches a page for a user-initiated request. OpenAI says robots.txt rules may not apply to these requests.
Anthropic's crawler for public web data.
Indexes pages to improve search result quality for Claude users.
Fetches pages when a Claude user asks about them directly.
Not a crawler. A robots.txt token that controls whether Google may use your content for Gemini training and grounding. Blocking it does not affect Google Search rankings.
Controls whether pages Applebot crawled may be used to train Apple foundation models. Blocking it does not remove you from Siri and Spotlight.
Fetches pages during a user's live question. Perplexity says this user-triggered fetcher generally ignores robots.txt.
Builds the open Common Crawl dataset, which many AI labs use as training data. Blocking it affects every downstream user of the dataset, including researchers.
No public documentation. Widely reported as aggressive, and multiple operators have reported it ignoring robots.txt in the past, so a robots rule may not be enough on its own.
Improves Amazon products and services, and fetched content may be used to train Amazon AI models.
Builds search experiences in Amazon products such as Alexa. Amazon says it is not used for generative model training.
Fetches current web information for user actions such as Alexa questions. Amazon says it is not used for generative model training.
Collects data for Meta AI training and product improvement.
Fetches individual links for user-initiated requests. Meta says this fetcher may bypass robots.txt.
Crawls for open research models and datasets.
Reported by site operators in access logs. No official public documentation at the time of the last review.
